[IMAGE:0]It's getting on for midnight, so here we go with a new Magic Monday. The picture is of Florence Farr, a famous actress of the late 19th century British stage and a leading initiate of the Hermetic Order of the Golden Dawn. The instrument is a psaltery tuned in quarter tones, built by musicologist Arnold Dolmetsch, with which she used to accompany poetry readings by fellow GD adept William Butler Yeats. (Yeats' essay on the subject can be read online here.)

Ask me anything about occultism and I'll do my best to answer it. Any question received by midnight Monday Eastern time will get an answer, though it may be Tuesday sometime before I get to them all. If you're in a hurry, or suspect you may be the 128,715th person to ask a question, please check out the very rough version 1.0 of The Magic Monday FAQ here.

I've had several people ask about tipping me for answers here, and though I certainly don't require that I won't turn it down. You can use the button below to access my online tip jar.

With that said, have at it! 

***This Magic Monday is now closed to new questions. See you next week!***

comments.title: Magic in Cark Age Christendom
comments.content: My understanding is that (and I realize there are other issues involved in this question) St. Patrick permitted the use of some forms of divination and/or magic from Christian converts (eg, Ogham divination). This impression is born out by the survival, in the Stowe missal, of at least three magical charms (one against "thorns"). From other sources and research, I gather that the Christian Dark Ages were fairly tolerant towards magic, particularly of the benefic kind, provided they did not involve concourse with demons (eg., evocation, etc.). The date(s) I have worked out are around the turn of the millennia, and then through to the Inquisitorial times, for the real crackdown on "magic". I wondered if this squared with what you know on the subject?

replies.title: Re: Magic in Dark Age Christendom
replies.content: Your understanding is quite correct. I recommend Valerie Flint's excellent book The Rise of Magic in Early Medieval Europe as a solid overview of the widespread acceptance of Christian magic from the end of the Roman period through the High Middle Ages. The basic rule was that if it was beneficial magic, on the one hand, and called solely on God and the saints on the other, it was considered not merely acceptable but praiseworthy; it was only later on that things soured, and practices that had been seen as evidence of devout faith in one century became crimes for which you could get burnt at the stake in the next.

comments.title: Child's perception of people and color
comments.content:
My daughter is 2 years old. She has been assigning colors to people to the point that I wonder if it's possible she is really seeing these colors.

I first noticed that, if she has colorful objects (ex: blocks), she says that the blue is for Mommy, purple is for daddy, and pink is for herself. When those three colors are present, it will always be "assigned" by her in this way.

Next was her art. She draws mom with a blue crayon, dad with a purple, and herself with a pink. But instead of saying "Blue is for mommy" she says "Mommy is blue."

Then once when dad watched her drawing, he asked to be blue. Our daughter recoiled and was quite insistant that, "No. MOMMY is blue. Daddy IS NOT blue. Daddy is PURPLE. Her tone and manner got my attention. It was the first time I noticed how real this color thing was to her. It's the sort of tone she might have used if you tried calling a rabbit a duck.

Other details that may or may not matter:
1. My daughter's verbal development has always been very rapid. She has a decent grasp of grammar and is quite capable of expressing "X is for Y" and/or "X is Y" as she pleases. She also started verbally identifying her own emotions and expressing empathy quite early.
2. She says her great-aunt is green, with the same certainty as the previously mentioned colors.
3. BUT she is not always so sure. She'll say grandma is yellow, but usually hesitates/thinks about it before saying so. I've seen her use orange crayon for grandma, but mostly it's yellow.
4. Last week, out of curiousity, I asked my daughter what color is her babysitter. She thought about it a bit, "She issss....she isss...." before finally telling me "I don't know what color [babysitter] is." Then immediately followed up with "Mommy, ask me what color [babysitter's sister] is!"
"Okay," I said, "What color is she?"
"[Babysitter's sister] is BLUE!"
This exchange really got my attention. She was very against letting dad be blue, but totally fine saying this other person is blue. Then she followed up a minute later by saying her babysitter "likes pink." (Not "is" pink, like everyone else "is" their color.)
5. These colors have stayed consistent. She is always sure of the ones she's sure of. She always looks thoughtful before saying grandma is yellow. And she has yet to say that her babysitter IS any particular color, but has mentioned twice more the color her babysitter LIKES.
I have not asked her about other people's colors, and she has not brought up anyone else.
6. In case it's relevant, we do not practice magic, though my husband and I are very devoted in spiritual/religious areas.(Our extended families less so. I come from a mixed background, both in terms of ethnicity and religion. Husband has a protestant background.) I am only superficially familiar with things related to the occult. I came across something of yours describing the raspberry jam principle, which explained so much of what I've witnessed with people I've known to be involved with magic, and have since been reading your blog and learning a little more.

In closing, I realize that this color business can be explained as a child's imagination and quirkiness. Nevertheless I wonder, is there a more spiritual/magical explanation that seems to fit? If so:
1. What are the colors she sees? Energy?
2. Is this thought to be reasonably common for toddlers?
3. Does this give me any particular insight about my child?
4. Is there insight to be gleaned from the colors themselves? [Herself=pink, mom=blue, dad=purple, great-aunt=green, grandma=yellow(ish?), sitter's sister=blue]
5. Why might she be less clear on some people than others? Is there potential cause for concern that the sitter's color is not identifiable by my child? I have known the sitter and her family for over a decade and the relationship between our families is very good.

Thank you for your time and consideration. I appreciate your opening up this forum for (potentially quite silly) questions like mine.

replies.title: Re: Child's perception of people and color

replies.title: Re: Child's perception of people and color
replies.content:
1. In all probability your child has some clairvoyant ability and is seeing the primary colors of people's auras.

2. It's very common. Most children have some degree of natural psychism, until they're bullied into silencing it by a society that insists on treating it as "just imagination."

3. Not that I know of. It does speak well of her parents that she feels comfortable talking about her perceptions to you.

4. Hard to say. Auric vision is a very personal thing; different people perceive the colors differently.

5. She may not have an adequate range of color-words -- if the sitter's aura is a color for which she doesn't have a name, that would explain it. Her auric sense may also stray into colors that aren't in the normal visual spectrum. It's nothing to be concerned with; as your child gets older and develops a more sophisticated vocabulary, she's likely to be able to say, "well, it's kind of a tawny gold with a little bit of green in it," or something like that -- the kind of fine verbal distinctions two-year-olds aren't very good at.

Your question isn't silly at all, by the way. Thank you for caring enough about your daughter to be willing to explore the possibility that her perceptions are real; too many parents aren't willing to do that.

replies.title: Re: Child's perception of people and color
replies.content:
Sounds like synaesthesia to me. I am mildly synaesthetic. I have never had the association of colors with people, and certainly wouldn't rule out seeing auras :) but I have heard of others with this type. I also don't think that synaesthesia is an entirely "non-magical" phenomenon, for lack of a better term (just because something is catalogued by science, doesn't mean it's an entirely material phenomenon! It's not much studied, and science doesn't have a good explanation for it). I believe there is some connection with things that are real, but not seen by everyone. The one thing we do know about synaesthesia is that people who have it tend to have better-than-average memory and recall.

Regarding #2) There is a theory that everybody starts out this way, and that you lose it as your brain matures and prunes out unnecessary connections. Some of us just have an inefficient neuron-pruning process and never completely lose this. So: "most people grow out of it"-- but so far this theory is not testable.

Regarding #4) synaesthetic associations are highly idiosyncratic, and I wouldn't read too much into them. My color associations are mostly with numbers, and to a lesser extent, letters-- grapheme synaesthesia. When I compare notes with other synaesthetics, there is usually some overlap, but mostly not. We often disagree about the color of particular graphemes. So the individual color she associates with a person may have more to do with her own personal associations with that color, rather than some more universal association with that color (does that make sense?).

Regarding #5) I wouldn't worry about this. In my experience, not all letters and numbers have have equally strong colors, and some of them are transparent, and some of them sort of flip back and forth between black or white and colorless. So, like, A is the strongest. It is red. In a word that starts with A, the word is red no matter what the other letters are, because the A overpowers them. I is transparent. If you had asked me, at age 4, what color it was, I would not have been able to tell you. It's elusive, and I probably didn't have the vocabulary for it at the time. My own name IRL is marigold orange-- sort of between yellow and orange. I would not have been able to tell you, as a child, whether that was yellow or orange ;) Not all synaesthetic colors exist in the physical world. Those would be pretty much impossible for a young child to describe. IMO, if she's not sure about a color, it might be because she doesn't have the means to describe it to you.

This is all, of course, personal opinion, for what it's worth.

comments.title: Inari Update and questions about why sacred languages and advice for someone who "Wants to Believe"
comments.content:
Hi JMG!

It's me, J again, the Inari guy from the last Magic Monday.
I prayed before going to bed, asking Inari Okami if it was indeed the case that they were saying they were available to have a relationship as a kami. I woke up the next morning and found:

2) Someone on facebook (a person, rather than a served ad) posting a picture of an amusing glass that said "For [picture of fox] sake"
3) Weiser Book's Tarot Tuesday, a card from the "The Druid Animal Oracle Deck" by Philip & Stephanie Carr-Gomm. Of course it was The Fox.

I decided I'd see where this goes, and learned about praying toward Kyoto (home of Fushimi Inari shrine, the central Inari shrine) from one of the admins, and tried doing that morning and night. I ordered a book of norito (Shinto prayers) with the original Classical Japanese, romaji transcription, and English translation, which includes an Inari prayer. Not long after, I got it, and started practicing it, which was pretty humbling. So much stumbling on the words. I had wanted to study and learn some Japanese years ago, but never did and I seriously regret that now. But I still did it and do it. Thursday morning, I did the bowing and the clapping and read the norito as best as I could and got hit with this sense of awe and reverence and peace that I hadn't felt in years that just silenced my mental and emotional noise.

So it has been an emotionally dizzying time, with feelings bubbling up that I been avoiding engaging the language and spirituality due to fear of difficulty as well as a degree of self-consciousness I though I had banished long ago. The causes:

1)Fear of the toxic and historically illiterate quasi-racism that attempts to police cultural exchange and religious calling. Someone close to me has faced the prospect of that possibility in Orisha worship where they live.

2)Fear of difficulty in proper practice (and in the case of language, learning even a little Japanese)

3)Happening to like anime and manga and feeling like that somehow automatically makes engagement with Inari or any other kami superficial.

The Inari Faith group on Facebook has helped me a lot with that simply by existing. Thank you so much for this.

This has also made me decide to apply myself to my previous commitments as well, by going back to the basics with the Egyptian goddess Hathor, Netjeru willing.

Sacred Languages: What gives a language spiritual power when spoken or chanted in the original rather than in translation? The norito book mentions this with respect to the Classical Japanese norito (so I read them aloud in my stumbling Classical Japanese). I have read the same about Sanskrit, about the Egyptian language (which makes the lack of written vowels frustrating), and Hebrew.

"I Want to Believe": I spoke about my Inari stuff to a close friend who is not religious, but is intellectually open to the spiritual. He said he wished he could experience something like what I've experienced. He said he (metaphorically) has Fox Mulder's "I Want To Believe" poster on his wall, as in he wants to, but can't make the jump.

I told him that he needs to do stuff to have more opportunities for something to happen. Just as if you want to see more foxes, you need to go outside more. And I suggested he pray to the deity he had an interest in and seeing what happens. Do you have anything additional, anything that might help my friend?

-J

replies.title: Re: Inari Update and questions about why sacred languages and advice for someone who "Wants to Belie
replies.content:
Delighted to hear this, It sounds to me as though you've taken some serious first steps.

Which collection of norito do you have?

Okay, on to the questions:

1) That's complex, since languages are complex. Sound is important, meaning is important, and exactly which is more important depends on the tradition. It's a standard Shinto teaching that you need to recite the norito in classical Japanese, so it's best to trust that the people who hold to that tradition know what they're talking about. You can find any number of free introductory courses in Japanese online; consider using those resources to get some basic familiarity with the sounds and structure of the modern language, and go from there.

2) Belief is overrated. That word "belief" simply means having an opinion, and your opinions simply don't matter that much when it comes to spiritual phenomenon. Encourage your friend to stop worrying about belief -- does a scientist decide in advance what result of his experiment he believes in? Of course not. Instead, take the existence of deities as a working hypothesis, and approach prayer and other religious activities with a genuinely open mind, neither believing nor disbelieving, but letting the results happen the way they happen. That would be my advice, certainly!

replies.title: Re: Inari Update and questions about why sacred languages and advice for someone who "Wants to Belie
replies.content:
"Sacred languages" (aka "sacral languages," "ritual languages") are a real phenomenon all over the world. They are not limited to high civilizations with old traditions of writing. They are quite common--maybe close to universal--in aboriginal cultures that did not traditionally use writing at all. Like mundane languages, sacral languages all have their own proper rules of grammar and their own extensive vocabularies. (In cultures without writing, of course, these grammars and vocabularies have not been written down, but they are just as normative as the same things are in languages that use writing.)

Anthropological linguists have been studying sacral languages, on and off, for more than a century now. As yet, alas! there is no good, comprehensive, survey of the phenomenon across the globe. Nor do we have even the faint beginnings of a good theory why such languages should be so wide-spread.

However, anything so wide-spread almost certainly has very deep roots in our common human meat-ware and wet-ware. This means that sacral languages can indeed be used by every sort of magician to good effect. Unsurprisingly, magicians have actually used sacral languages as far back as our records of magic go.

Glossolalia, or "speaking in tongues," is almost as common in many cultures all over the globe. (It is most definitely older than Christianity, and not limited to that religion.) Most likely there is some deep connection between the two phenomena. But no one yet knows enough about either phenomenon to work it out.

comments.title: Karma, born with cognitive disabilities, magical cure?
comments.content:
The son of a friend of mine was recently born and diagnosed with a chromosomal disorder that affects cognitive and physical functions (down syndrome). As you can imagine,it's been a tough time and everyone is still trying to make sense of it. What do you believe is the karmic reason for such afflictions?

replies.title: Re: Karma, born with cognitive disabilities, magical cure?
replies.content:
Okay, first of all, it's crucial to be aware that the medical industry has a business model that involves turning any such condition into a lifelong dependency on pharmaceuticals and professional services. Your friend's son is entirely capable of leading a happy, relatively normal life. Let me tell you a story from my family tree...

One of my paternal great-great-grandmothers had Down syndrome or, as they called it in those days, Mongolism. She had all the classic signs -- the red hair, the short fingers, all the rest of it, and she had certain intellectual limits. On the other hand, she grew up in Washington Territory with a farm family who loved her and simply made adjustments for her condition. She never learned to write her own name; on the other hand, she became an extremely capable cook and housekeeper, and adored children; when my great-great-grandfather met and married her, he considered himself to be the luckiest man alive. She had a long and happy life, and my grandfather as a boy and young man learned how to cook from her; he went on to join the Aberdeen, WA fire department, and earned a reputation as the best firehouse cook in southwestern Washington.

The moral to this story is that your friend's son isn't condemned to the kind of helplessness that the medical community likes to insist is inevitable with Down syndrome (or any other congenital syndrome involving cognitive deficits -- the crap they say about Aspergers syndrome these days, which I have, makes my blood boil). I'd strongly encourage you to help your friends find a support group for Down syndrome parents that focuses on helping Down syndrome kids to become as independent and capable as they can, and to find whatever talents they have -- many, many people with Downs syndrome turn out to have unexpected gifts in this or that field. If his family gives him plenty of love and support, he can have as happy a life as my ancestress did, without institutionalization, and without being drugged into a stupor by an industry more interested in its profits than its patients.

Now for your questions. The karma of congenital conditions is complex, and can't be reduced to a one-size-fits all scheme. Clearly the experience of Down syndrome is one your friends' son needs to have; exactly why, and how that will work out, would require the sort of detailed past life knowledge that human beings can't usually access.

As for magic, I'd start by doing a series of Rose+Cross rituals for his benefit -- you know how to do those for someone absent, don't you? If not, let me know and I'll walk you through it. Cast and interpret his horoscope, or get it done by a good astrologer if you don't have that skill set yet, and look for impairments to the sixth house ruler or any planet in the sixth house, also for third house issues. On that basis you can look at creating and consecrating a talisman for the child's benefit -- I think it was Regardie who pointed out that talismanic magic is the art of horoscope repair. More important than anything else, though, is to encourage your friends to give their son all the love and encouragement they would give any other child, and don't buy into the propaganda that insists that any child with a disability is broken and defective. Their support and love is a more powerful magic than anything you can cast.

replies.title: Re: Karma, born with cognitive disabilities, magical cure?
replies.content:
I want to second JMG's comments here.

One of the uglier problems of the 20th century was the conscious decision that children born with Down's Syndrome and other forms of mental disabilities are basically human waste, too impaired to ever lead decent lives, and incapable of ever learning anything. That led to a couple of generations of people with Down's and similar conditions being incarcerated from infancy in institutions where they were ignored and left to rot.

Once people began in the last quarter of the 20th century to advocate for the education of kids with Downs and other mental impairments, scientists finally realized that the vast majority of children born with Downs Syndrome have IQs not all that far below normal, and are completely capable of leading happy, fulfilled lives --- if they are loved, cared for, and taught all of the things that mentally normal children are taught. They can hold down jobs, live independently, marry, have children (and parent their children competently), just like anyone else.

Please, please, please encourage your friends to treat their child as having every potential to live, learn, and grow. Yes, they'll need to accommodate his condition, but if they treat him as intelligent, capable of learning, and worth their time and effort, he'll grow up a much batter, happier, more functional man than if they treat him as less-than.

The Scottish author Jane Duncan wrote a series of children's about the Camerons--her actual nephews and niece given adventures of mild mystery and adventure. comments.title: Sense of lack
comments.content:
I recently realised, not entirely without help, that one of my defining personality traits is a sense of always lacking something. No matter how much I achieve, It is never enough. Not in a greed sense, more in the sense that everything I achieve becomes meaningless. This has led me to self sabotage in my career, relationships and my general well-being.

So my question is, instead of summoning abundance, success, which would consist of asking for something, is there a way to banish this sense of ‘lacking’ or discontent?

replies.title: Re: Sense of lack
replies.content:
Okay, good. You've taken the crucial step by realizing that the feeling of a lack is a psychological trait in you rather than something in the world around you. That's crucial, and it's something many people will do absolutely anything in their power to avoid.

Now you need to chase the feeling of lack down to its roots. You can't just banish it, you need to understand where it comes from and why it has so deep a place in your psyche. Very often what's behind such feelings is some knotted mess of childhood experiences, and it's when you work your way down to those and deal with the emotions they stirred up that you can let go of the feeling and face the world in terms that aren't poisoned by childhood miseries.

How do you do that? Journaling, as explained in the Magic Monday FAQ, can be a very useful tool here. You can also simply ask yourself the question, "What in my childhood does this feeling remind me of?" -- and be ready to do this again and again, as it's rare for a deep-seated emotional complex like yours to be rooted in a single experience. You're probably going to be reliving some really wretched experiences, but it's like cleaning out an infected wound: it hurts, but then you can heal.

One other thing. A sense that one's achievements are always inadequate is very often rooted in one or both parents' always treating whatever you did as inadequate. If that's the case with you, you may have to let yourself feel just how brutally abusive it is to treat a child that way...

comments.content: How does relocational astrology work?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Depends on what you mean by that. If you mean why does it work, nobody knows why astrology in general works, and we'll have to figure that out before the subdisciplines can be explained.

If you want to know how it's done, on the other hand, that's easy enough. There are various approaches. The one I've used with reliable success is to cast a natal chart for the same moment you were born -- correcting for changes in time zone -- but for the place you're considering moving to. Each planet will be in the same degree of the same sign as in your natal chart, but its house placement will be different. You're looking for a place where your strong planets are where you want them to be -- say, in your tenth house if you want career success, or what have you -- while your problem planets are rotated into houses that don't matter much to you, or into the cadent houses (3rd, 6th, 9, or 12th), which weaken their effect.

Here's an example. On the west coast, my natal Saturn is angular -- that is to say, very close to one of the four angles of my chart -- and therefore very strong. replies.title: Re: Geomantic chart
replies.content:
If I may take a quick stab at this...

Magic is normally the 8th house, not the 9th. Thus the house chart denies perfection. Noting the figures in those houses, Cauda Draconis in the 8th emphasizes the need for completion of what you've already begun in this regard. Your own significator, Carcer, shows you in a state of unproductive tension due to being pulled in multiple opposite directions at once.

replies.title: Re: Geomantic chart
replies.content:
Okay, before we go on, where did you get the info that there are no SOL lodges in the US? I ask this because (a) I know members of three such lodges, and (b) it took me about a minute and a half to visit the SOL website and find out that there are currently SOL lodges in Northern Virginia; Atlanta, Georgia; Portland, OR; Littleton, CO; and Seattle, WA. Furthermore, the SOL course of study isn't at all identical to the course Knight presents in Experience of the Inner Worlds -- it's based largely on WE Butler's work, as expanded and reworked by Dolores Ashcroft-Nowicki. (Knight used to run a correspondence course based on the book, but as far as I know it's been closed to students for quite a few years now.) One very useful habit for divination is to make sure that what you think you know is actually so...

Okay, now let's proceed to the chart. When you ask a house chart a yes or no question, the answer is determined by the presence or absence of a mode of perfection connecting the figure of the querent with that of the quesited. Magic corresponds to the eighth house, spirituality to the ninth; a case could be made for either one...and that's complex, because you've got a sextile to the ninth but a square to the eighth. That's also the only mode of perfection you have, and a chart that perfects only by aspect is, in my experience, a very weak yes at best. Since both your significator and that of the quesited are difficult figures, it's a *very* weak yes, and Rubeus in the 9th suggests that you may well be deceiving yourself about your attraction to the GD system, and will end up dumping it for something else in due time (Amissio in the 4th).

It's doubtless no accident that your significator, Carcer, is in company capitular with the figure in the second house, Puer. That is to say, you feel stuck, and your response is to want to rush off to something else you think you would like more. Notice, however, that this opens up yet another aspect, because Puer passes to the fifth, square to the 8th house but trine to the 9th. The company capitular also offers good advice, though: stick with what you're doing for now (Carcer) and when you're done with it, move on decisively to something else (Puer).

More generally, may I offer one piece of ironclad advice for magical training? Finish what you start.. The strength of will you'll gain by sticking with something all the way through will confer far more benefits than anything else you can do.

replies.title: Re: Geomancy regarding moving across town

comments.title: Shadow work
comments.content:
I fear acknowledging repressed thoughts and feelings because I don't know if I would be reinforcing them or not if I did.

I feel I have some deep hate and anger inside of me, desire for violence, and I want to look at it more but worry I will just become it more by doing so.

replies.title: Re: Shadow work
replies.content:
The longer you repress something, the stronger it gets, because the energy you put into repression feeds the thing you repress. If you want the energy you've locked up in these feelings to come unstuck and flow into more balanced forms, you've got to accept the reality of them as natural parts of your psyche, and that means you have to face them, acknowledge them as yours, accept that this is also part of who you are.

Hatred and anger are natural, normal human emotions, and it's as bad an idea to repress them as it is to repress sexual desire. You can acknowledge your sexual desires without having to act on them all the time; in the same way, you can acknowledge your hatred and anger without having to act on them all the time -- and there are things that it's worthwhile to hate and times when anger really is the best response. It's by repressing them so violently in recent decades, insisting that they have no place at all in the spectrum of human emotions, that our society has become so viciously passive-aggressive and so enamored of the sort of bland plastic niceness that so often turns into covert cruelty. Honest hate and open anger are far less toxic than these.

comments.title: Pluto Uranus
comments.content:
Hi JMG

I am one of those 60s babies with a Pluto Uranus conjunction. It's in my first house so it feels personal, not just collective. But how does one integrate 2 transpersonal planets? Currently transiting Pluto and Saturn are trining it so now feels like a good time to try to reach some kind of accommodation with this conjunction (gods).

I read that the ancient Greeks were not allowed to worship Pluto / Hades - it that true? I notice there is an Orphic hymn to him anyway but not to Uranus, the original sky father. Liz Greene mentioned that if we don't allow some room for the gods in our lives, they can make an appearance anyway in some very unwelcome ways. What do you suggest I do?

Thanks!

replies.title: Re: Pluto Uranus
replies.content:
There is indeed an Orphic hymn to Uranus -- the Thomas Taylor translation gives it as a hymn to Heaven, but you tell me who's being addressed here:

"GREAT Heav'n, whose mighty frame no respite knows,
Father of all, from whom the world arose:
Hear, bounteous parent, source and end of all,
Forever whirling round this earthly ball;
Abode of Gods, whose guardian pow'r surrounds
Th' eternal World with ever during bounds;
Whose ample bosom and encircling folds
The dire necessity of nature holds.
Ætherial, earthly, whose all-various frame
Azure and full of forms, no power can tame.
All-seeing Heav'n, progenitor of Time,
Forever blessed, deity sublime,
Propitious on a novel mystic shine,
And crown his wishes with a life divine."

Hades was also worshiped in Greek times, though the rites were usually secret, and addressed him as Ploutos, "the Rich One." So you can do the traditional thing, burn some incense to each of them, recite the corresponding hymn, and then enter into attentive silence for a time.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
Hi JMG,

Recently on a Magic Monday there have been some questions/replies about karma and how you can "invite" karma-clearing into your life and then all kinds of bad things happen to you for quite some time. Without going into too much detail, there were some spiritual experiences I held very close to me throughout my youth, until I was about 30, that brought a lot of magic and wonder to my life -- a lot of synchronicity and beautiful moments. Then, at 30, I found myself in a group of friends who were all "normal" and fairly atheist, and who definitely tended to mock those who had any kind of religious/spiritual beliefs. I got pulled into this (it was nice to have friends after a long time alone) and after some mental convolutions I abandoned all that I'd once believed in, for about nine years, and took to being as skeptical, and sometimes even as mocking, as my peers.

Anyway, nine years later a personal crisis lead me back to some of those old important things, and I cautiously invited them back into my life, mostly through writing. Then the dreams came again and then all the little magic synchronicities and beautiful moments. But at the same time, a lot of Really Bad Stuff began to happen, the worst of it involving the breakdown of several friendships/family relationships, a giant career-check for myself, and then a load of other hassles, health concerns, etc. that had not been present previously. It has been three years of clearing out and rebuilding.

I'm wondering now if this is "regular karma" I had just put aside for nine years while I lived a fairly spirit-less life, or if I am paying back for that period of time when I ignored what is really so integral to my being. It has lightened up in the past months since I began wading in the waters here, and the good bits are certainly worth all the trouble, but I'm wondering if I could have known what was waiting for me when I said to myself, "Oh, yes, I think I'll start writing about that again." Like was that a kind of invitation for all of this to happen?

It seems like so many people who live very far away from any spirituality also tend to live very steady, mundane lives. Everyone has the occasional catastrophe, of course, but do we become more likely to experience these things the closer we get to experiencing a higher state of being? Or is it sometimes just a hurdle we have to get past?

I would really like to think of it as a hurdle at this point and have a bit more of the good stuff ahead. ;)

Thanks as always,
Diane

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: It's a hurdle. The old occult books call it "karmic culmination" -- before you can get moving on the spiritual path, you need to clear away old business, and yeah, that generally sucks for a while. On the other hand, once it's done with, the way is clear and you can move forward. What makes the lives of the unspiritual look calmer is that most of them are stuck in lifelong ruts.

replies.title: Re: Individuation
replies.content: Jung sounds like an occultist because he was an occultist. The Self is precisely his version of the Higher Self aka Holy Guardian Angel. replies.title: Re: Individuation
replies.content:
Jung's first major publication was a study of what happens in a seance when some being other than the Medium speaks through the Medium. (In general, this is a fascinating question, the answer to which varies from one instance to the next, and often remains somewhat indeterminate.) As our host noted about Jung's work in general, it is occultism dressed up as scientific psychology.

Jung's own private library, which is still extant, has many works on occultism and psychical research. He even owned a copy of the rare first (1899) edition of C. G. Leland's "Aradia; or the Gospel of the Witches."

comments.title: Lost items
comments.content:
I recently lost a lovely wool shawl and a book (not just any book, either - 'The Conspiracy Book' !!!) I tried backtracking at places I've visited but no luck. Is there any natural magic that can help me find these items?

Oddly enough, I seem to have a gift for helping others find lost items even at a distance (some times even specifically, i.e. "you'll find in the back left corner of the top left desk drawer"). This gift or sense just doesn't work for me. Sigh.

replies.title: Re: Lost items
replies.content: It's actually quite common for people to be able to practice psychism for other people but not for themselves. Sorry to hear about the loss!

replies.title: Re: Lost items
replies.content:
For the shawl, since it is an item you wore and are fond of, you have a magical link with it and you could make use of that link to locate it. One way would be to address it aloud or mentally as you would a sentient being such as a lost pet. "I miss you. Are you all right? Where are you? Do you want to come home?" and see whether you get a return mental message either while waking or in your dreams. When you contact the shawl, you could also ask it to call out to you or give you some kind of "You're getting warmer," signal if/when you happen to be somewhere in the vicinity.

I would also try divination. The most obvious tool would be a pendulum, which is good for yes/no questions and for dowsing locations on a map. If you aren't practiced with a pendulum, whatever divination method you use can work. You want to narrow down the possibilities for both objects. Some of the pertinent yes/no questions would be whether the object is in someone's possession, is it in plain sight or hidden, has it been discarded, has it been moved away from the area where you lost it. If it hasn't been thrown away, you could ask a series of nearer/farther questions with reference to some specific location.

It's possible that you could amplify your object locating gifts by using a deck of Lenormand cards. One of the cards in the deck is "Book" and has a book on it. You could use that card as a significator and draw another card at random; the shape and associations of the picture on the drawn card may give a clue as to where to look. Lenormand decks are fairly cheap but you could also make a deck if you have access to a printer or can draw a little. There are Lenormand deck images online and you could print them out and glue them on light card stock or just draw the symbols. The symbols are basic pictures like a key, a house, mice, bouquet of flowers, etc. You can also do Lenormand divination with a deck of ordinary playing cards if you print out the key of which card in a regular deck corresponds with each card in the Lenormand deck (which has fewer than 52 cards).

If you decide to try one of these methods and it works for you, I hope you will post something about it on a Monday.
(Deborah Bender)

comments.title: Unexpected Dream
comments.content:
The main question I have is about a dream recounted to me by my younger sister. It was vivid and startled her. I have dreams like that too, as does my other sister. It feels like it takes energy out of me when I have these types of dreams. When I was younger I would wake up shaking and unable to speak for a while. Sometimes they seem to be about things in the future, other times they are things I am not sure what to make of. They just happen and I can't control when. I know that my great great grandmother predicted her brother's death and how it would happen shortly before it did, this was attested by multiple family members. My great grandmother had something similar, she lived a very long life and had a dream like this that came to pass in my lifetime. My grandmother has had them, but she tends to discount them most of the time. My mother has strange dreams sometimes too. It may be a familial thing. I have been interested in this for most of my life and collect every data point I can on it.

The content of my sister's dream itself was not too scary(except for a large and aggressive dog) or anything like that. What she described is what startled me. It sounded like a point by point description of the underworld(or otherworld) found in many traditions. It was close to the Germanic Hel(from broader sources than just the Icelandic ones, like the story of Hadding). I am a heathen and widely read on religious topics and folklore in general, so this immediately came to mind. My sister has little to no knowledge of this stuff, so I discounted the dream being a result of something put together from things she had on her mind. I explained this similarity to her when she asked me about the dream. It was one of those strange types of dream too, so its details stuck with her.

The basic sequence of the dream was that she started out on the coast of a dark sea. One thing she noted about the whole atmosphere is that it was unnaturally peaceful and quiet. That and space was kind of strange. She walked down a path inland from this sea, there was a big dog that scared her, but a man came and told her that he would guide her. They walked through a forest, she noted here that it almost seemed like the trees along the path were scrunching inward toward the road. There was some marshy land, and then they came to a river with a stone bridge. She noted houses with what she described as "old fashioned farming tools". The man guiding her would not cross the bridge, and warned her that she ought not to cross it, but to leave. There was a woman outside the house near the bridge. Some people were coming up to cross the bridge. She then woke up.

Do you have any idea what this dream might mean if it has any significance at all?

replies.title: Re: Unexpected Dream
replies.content: Good question. A lot depends on what happens next -- whether your sister experiences the awakening of psychic gifts, for example. It may be just a dream, or more than that; time will tell.

comments.content:
Happy Monday, JMG-

To what extent does the Golden Dawn involve/consider the Christian deity as a "personal" God, versus as a symbolic system describing aspects of the universe?

It seems to me that in broader occult thought, these are generally separate ideas- Hermeticists and occultists in general often consider the Infinite, or the cosmos, to be a person in some sense (such as the Kybalion's concept of the "All", or the idea of the highest spiritual plane as a universal consciousness), but they wouldn't consider the particular deity of Judeo-Christian mythology to be identical with this personage. It's not clear to me what the original architects of the GD thought about this distinction, or Who in particular they were concerned with in their symbolism.

In slightly more concrete terms; the words of the Cabalistic Cross are in the form of an address ("thou art the kingdom...", etc), presumably to the same being-or-otherwise named by the Divine Names and so on. Should the practicioner consider themselves to be addressing the Universe? Or addressing some being that exists within the universe? (Or something else?)

-A

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Depends on the Golden Dawnie! To me, the claim that the transcendent ground of being is also a person is not so much wrong as incomprehensible -- it's rather like saying "colorless green ideas sleep furiously," or insisting that 1 + 1 = the scent of violets. One of these things is not like the other! There are people, though, who disagree with that. I'd encourage you to take it as a theme for meditation.
